content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF

NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY                                770 L STREET, SUITE 800                    SAN FRANCISCO
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000             SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814              650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941           TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                 SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                                                         TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800


                                    October 15, 1991                             FPPC
                                                                                 OCT 16 1991

Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

     Re:  Architects as Public Officials ("Consultants")

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

     We are requesting advice on behalf of a client which is an
architectural firm regarding the possible status of the firm's
architects as public official/"consultants" under the Political
Reform Act (Gov. Code § 82048, 2 CCR § 18720(a)(2).) Based on
the Commission's Rupp Advice (No. 79-109), we have long
understood that architects who work on specific projects for
public entities pursuant to a contract for architectural services
are not "consultants" who are "public officials" under the Act.
We wish to confirm that understanding.

     Our client is a corporation owned by two licensed
architects, each of whom is a 50% shareholder. Some of the
firm's architectural services are provided to the State of
California pursuant to "New School Projects" agreements, wherein
a school district, as agent for the State Allocation Board,
contracts with an architect or architectural firm to design and
monitor the progress on the construction of a particular school,
which is identified by name in the contract. A copy of the
standard form "New School Project" contract is attached and
incorporated by reference in this request. The form has been in
use since 1984 and is still used today.

     The vast majority of school districts in the state do not
have "on staff" architects who design and monitor school
construction. Because the services of an architect are not
necessary unless the school district is building a school,
architectural services of the type described in the attached
agreement are customarily rendered pursuant to contracts with
outside firms. It is our understanding that since 1984,
thousands of the new schools constructed in California have been
funded by the State of California through the Leroy F. Greene
State School Building Lease Purchase Law of 1976 referred to in
the attached agreement. On all of these "New School Projects,"
architectural firms have signed agreements identical to the
attached. The standard New School Project agreement for

Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991
Page 2


architects was designed by the Office of Local Assistance, a
subdivision of the State Allocation Board, under the aegis of the
Department of General Services, which is the ultimate
administrator of the New School Project Lease-Purchase Program.
No variations in the terms of the agreement are permitted.
Architectural firms which enter into New School Project
Agreements are paid on a standard schedule which has been in use
for at least five years.

Although the "architect" with whom the particular school
district contracts on behalf of the State in a New School Project
agreement may be a firm employing one or more licensed
architects, state law governing architectural services requires
that for each structure such as a new school, a single "architect
of record" sign design drawings and other documents required in
the performance of professional services. Thus, of the two
principals in the architectural firm we represent, only one will
function as architect of record for a particular New School
Project. The selection of the particular natural person within
the firm who will serve as "architect of record" is made by the
architectural firm.

It is our understanding that the Commission has heretofore
not regarded an architect who renders only services such as
design and monitoring to a public agency as a "public
official/consultant," as those terms are used in Government Code
sections 82048, 87100, 87302, and Title 2, section 18700 of the
California Code of Regulations. Our understanding is based on
past staff advice. For example, in 1979 the Commission advised
that when an architectural firm contracted to make design
proposals to a redevelopment agency, the firm's architects were
not public officials under the Act. The Commission staff
reasoned the architects were engaged to design a specific project
and were not rendering or giving ongoing advice. Thus, the
architects could recommend to the public agency a design
compatible with that used by the developer who had employed them
on an adjacent private project, even though the compatible design
of public buildings could possibly enhance the value of the
private nearby condominiums owned by their private employer.
(Rupp Advice, supra. A copy of this letter, and the request on
which it is based, is attached.)

The analysis in Rupp is similar to that used in subsequent
FPPC advice letters to other professionals. For example, the
staff has advised that when a law firm is retained by a public
entity to draft a particular contract or handle a specific
lawsuit, the firm's attorneys are not consultants who are public

Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991
Page 3


officials under the Act; rather, as with many other vendors of
services, they have been engaged to deliver a "product" -- that
is, representation to achieve the resolution of a particular
legal dispute or matter. However, when a law firm is retained to
give ongoing advice on a variety of subjects, the attorneys are
public officials, as they are serving as quasi-public agency
staff counsel. (Lara Advice, No. I-90-277.) Here, an
architectural firm is retained to provide standardized architectural
services with respect to a particular New School -- not to advise
and consult generally. The architect under a New School
Agreement in effect contracts to deliver a product -- that is a
New School, certified by the architect to be complete. (See
Agreement, II D.4., final clause.)

Under a New School agreement, the client public agency
retains government powers to itself; the architect's function is
to provide independent, professional judgment. Standard
architectural services on a New School Project include advice and
consultation with the client on utilization of the particular
site based on review of the needs to be met in the particular New
School, (Agreement, II A.1.); assistance in obtaining permits and
approvals such as building and site access permits, where
necessary (Agreement, II B.3.); and input in preparing
specifications on bids for the prime building contract
(Agreement, II C.1.). Of course, the client must approve all New
School plans, and the State alone, and not the architect, awards
the actual building contract.

The services provided by an architectural firm on a New
School Project are similar to those rendered by a law firm in
litigating a particular matter, where the firm may make
recommendations to the client about the basis for a lawsuit, the
type of discovery to be conducted and the expense entailed, and
the advisability of accepting or rejecting a settlement offer.
However, lawyers in the firm offer their professional judgment
about how to achieve the client's goals, and the client approves
only those suggestions with which it agrees. The architectural
firm is similarly limited to design only within the parameters
set by its client. Like litigation attorneys, who are free to
choose which court reporting or other support service the law
firm will use, architects on a New School Project have
been free to sub-contract for support services based on their own
independent business and professional judgment, free of Political
Reform Act conflict of interest concerns. (See Agreement, III.)
We believe this is entirely appropriate. The public agency
client has determined in advance the amount to be paid to the
architect for services, with the understanding that the architect

Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991
Page 4


will bear his own subcontracting expense on the particular New
School Project.

     Although the architect of a New School renders professional
services, the client determines the scope of the project and
reviews and approves the architect's recommendations.

     The client under a New School Project has its own
independent "project inspector" (Agreement II, D.4.) and is
ultimately responsible for decisions such as changes in design or
materials which may result in an increase in the overall costs of
the New School Project.  (Agreement II, D.4.)  ["Proposed change
orders for written approval of the client"].  An example of a
proposed "change order" is attached).  Even if a "change order"
proposed by the architect results in no change in the cost of the
project or the amount of the construction contract, the change
order must be approved by the Change Order Section of the State
office of Local Assistance.  The client's independent inspector
also signs "certificates of payment," certifying as to the stages
of the New School Project which have been completed, which
"certifies" the architect prepares monthly as part of his or
her basic architectural services.  The "certificates of payment"
are the basis on which monies from the State are released to the
New School contractor under the terms of the separate standard
agreement between the State Allocation Board (with the School
District as agent) and that contractor.  However, the
"certificates of payment" do not change the overall amount due
the contractor from the State; they determine only the timing of
the payments to the contractor.

     Although a New School Project is multi-phasic, it is
durational.  The relationship between the architect and the State
and School District agents terminates one year after issuance of
the architect's certificate of completion.  (Agreement II, D.5.)
The Commission has advised that even though a specific project
may be lengthy and may entail frequent consultation, when the
project involves professional services which are not merely an
extension of public agency staff services, the professionals
retained by the agency are not public official/consultants.
(Torres Advice, A-86-245).

     Based on past advice, such as the Rupp and other letters, it
would appear that the "architect of record" in an architectural
firm retained by a public agency to render professional services
for a New School Project, and to provide architectural services
only in conjunction with that Project, is not a "public
official."  However, we have been asked to request confirmation

Mr. Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
October 15, 1991
Page 5


of that general principle. Therefore, we would appreciate your
response to the following questions:

Questions

1. Is the "architect of record" whose firm has entered into
a standard "New School Project" agreement (as attached) a
"consultant" who is a "public official" within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act, and thus subject to economic interest
disclosure and disqualification?

2. If the "architect of record" is a "consultant" who is a
"public official," which public agency designates the "architect
of record" as a "consultant" under its conflict of interest code,
or amends its existing code to cover architects on New School
Projects? The Department of General Services? The State
Allocation Board? Or the School District which acts as the
State's agent with respect to the particular Project?

We would appreciate a response to our inquiry at your
earliest convenience. We understand that Deanne Stone is the
attorney on your staff most familiar with these issues. Please
do not hesitate to call should you, Ms. Stone or any other member
of your staff need additional information regarding the
professional responsibilities of architects vis a vis New School
Projects.

Thank you,

[signature]
Judith Sproul Davis

JSD/kab

6641.01
